replies.author: @CurbYrDogma
replies.text:
I think everyone is missing various points. 

Going out on a limb, I'm guessing the directors of this movie might have assumed that the Russian Revolution was old news that wouldn’t interest the younger generations of today and were looking for a more contemporary spin. (When has there last been any Communist revolutions?) Perhaps they were seeing similarities between the hypocrisy shown in the original story and how authoritarians can rise to power under other systems as well.  Unfortunately, they took the wrong route and tried to “adapt” the original story. Bad move.

If they were trying to show parallels, then what they should have done is make a “sequel”, instead of using the original name and characters. That would have been a far better approach.

I take an interest in this because I was actually writing such a “sequel” (which was more closely aligned with the tone of the original) so was rather bummed out to see that this movie is coming out.  (Also it ruined that song for me…)

Observing that dictators can also rise to power under other systems is not a defense of Communism. And perhaps the movie's message is NOT that capitalism per se or all billionaires are "bad", but the power structure it shows might align more closely to what's going on right now in today's world.

It’s naive to think that authoritarian dictatorships can only happen under a Communist system and naive to think that a system opposite Communism (i.e. everything privatized) would never be oppressive. Both are unbalanced power structures. If one person or group gains too much power then that’s how we get dictatorships, no matter what kind of economic system. The key is balance, which is what mid-20th-century America was about.

I think that might be what the directors of this film were intending, and they were probably hoping for discussions like this, although the movie itself is an insult to the original story and a poorly-conceived cringe-fest filled with predictable comedy cliches.  I sincerely hope it flops and is forgotten about.

replies.text:
Maybe that is the whole point. You can view it on meta level, not directly - then it works wonderfully. Also, kids will never pick up Animal Farm these days... but bamboozling them to take it up after watching this, just to realize how bleak it is? That's actually not half bad a move.

And while the original works are against Stalin (Orwell was a socialist as well, he had nothing against the ideology, he had against totalitarian approach Stalin and Trotsky took), the messages that are threaded through all Orwell's work are not anti-socialist but anti-totalitarian. Which is why they are timeless.

Communism in the form described in the Manifesto is long dead, it is honestly not even a topic among serious people (except a few who use it as a dog whistle to hustle online), but totalitarian approach is pretty much universal. Basically every single party, every major activist group (left, right or center) is totalitarian - meaning their plan is to organize every aspect of society and not allow any other idea to exist.

If you look at US society today, both major parties are totalitarian to the extreme - they both try (and some succeed) to create a cult of personality of the leader, they both have extensive plans to organize and set every single thing in and around individual's life and they paint everyone else not thinking like them as a dangerous extremist that shouldn't be allowed to exist or at least be invisible in the societal sense (exist but quiet and inside their 4 walls).

That is what Orwell was warning about in all of his work, not any ideology in particular. Those come and go, usually within 2-3 generations as they are all set for a specific time and need in history. But our tendency to shift everything into totalitarianism - now THAT is a true problem.

But people read superficially and miss the most crucial point Orwell was making. They read that people in 1984 address each other as comrade, therefore it is criticism of communism/socialism and stop there.

So making Animal Farm in this way might actually be a good thing - to get the conversation started again and SOMEONE actually going back to read what the great man wrote instead of hate-harvesting online and being an idiot because it gathers likes and views.

replies.author: @IAMACollectivist
replies.text:
​@EmeraldSaphireSpiritBecause the impression OP gives is that Orwell was just another liberal in favor of capitalism and opposed to communism like the author. 

The truth is that Orwell was an anti-authoritarian socialist. He fought in Spain against the fascist Franko with the anarchist (socialist) poum militia. He was opposed to stalinism and capitalism. 

Animal farm is about the disappointment Orwell felt about the turn the Soviet union took under Stalin, but he also wasn't just critical of the crony side of capitalism. If you're updating animal farm for modern audiences decades after the fall of the Soviet union it probably does make sense to make capitalist tech bro utopianism the bad guy, but it also does sound like they screwed the pooch in their execution.

replies.author: @nickh5081
replies.text:
@IAMACollectivist  Orwell learned what a useless bunch of would-be-totalitarian iron fist rulers ALL leftists are at heart.  He saw what happened in the Soviet Union and hated it, he knew what a monster Stalin was.  His time in Spain taught him that they're all the same.  The Communists, Socialists and Anarchists started fighting for control of the ultra left before the war was even over (which helped Franco prevail).  

As for capitalism, Orwell supported capitalism whether he realized it or not, he just didn't want it to go unchecked and become the ruler of people - he wanted it held in check by democracy.  First and foremost Orwell believed in individual freedom, and as someone made wealthy by his own actions in a free market capitalist society, I guarantee he supported capitalism in theory if not exactly in the way it was being practiced during his lifetime.  My point being, the term "socialist" did not mean the same thing in England 80 to 100 years ago as it does today (wherever you are).

replies.text:
@kevinroche3334 
*"You are easily swayed - 1 film and you completely change your opinion of someone you have never met."*
Is this supposed to be satirical, or are you being serious? This could be a perfect example of Poe's Law...

Yes, it's actually perfectly reasonable for someone to change their opinion about someone after a single horrendous act or decision. And in this specific instance, we're talking about the *DIRECTOR* of this film. Serkis was the one tasked with putting his vision of the story onto the screen, and his vision of _Animal Farm_ is that the whole debacle at the Farm is *NOT* because the pigs became totalitarians when given power, but because _a greedy capitalist_ ruined it. In short, he's saying that not only was the United States responsible for the atrocities Stalin committed in the USSR, but that the United States _was responsible for putting Stalin in power in the first place._ That's the exact opposite of what Orwell was saying in the book, so, if Serkis believes he needs to co-opt _Animal Farm_ to sew anti-capitalist propaganda, then, yes, changing our opinion of him as a result is a perfectly reasonable thing to do. 

The choice to change our opinion of Serkis isn't indicative of being easily swayed, but I would argue choosing *NOT* to reevaluate your opinion of him after he has shown his colors indicates you are incapable of drawing conclusions for the evidence presented. As Dr. Maya Angelou said, "When someone shows you who they are, believe them the first time." Serkis is showing us who he really is.

The problem is, we already have two good adaptations already.

The first is the source of most of the images in this.  A 1954 animated movie made in England.  Then a second one on 1999 made in a style similar to the movie "Babe", but featuring Patrick Stewart as the voice of Napoleon, and Kelsey Grammer as Snowball (with Peter Ustinov as Old Major).

Other than the ending of those two, both stuck pretty damned closely to the original story.  The only notable change at all was at the very end where both gave it a "happy ending" where the Pigs were eventually overthrown and sanity returned without the corruption.  The original book ended when the rest of the animals looked in the window and realized that the pigs and humans all looked the same.

That’s exactly what critical drinker is trying to do with this video. He doesn’t understand the source material and doesn’t understand or know the authors stated intentions. 

Animal farm was a critique of authoritarianism not socialism. Critical drinker has got this wrong. George Orwell was a committed and self-described democratic socialist. replies.author: @XanderVJ
replies.text:
"Death of the Author" is one of the most putrid theories ever put to paper. Seriously, have you actually read the thing?

Basically it reduces the writer to little else than one of the proverbial million monkeys on typewriters, arguing that they are just but an arbitrary nexus of cultural and historical forces that just happen to put that in writing (and I kid you not) borderline "possessed" by the force of language itself.

Oh, and it's an intellectual argument for the destruction of the concept of copyright.

No, really. The last part of his essay is Barthes arguing that, since the author is not the primary force behind the creation and interpretation of a piece of work, actually, then it makes no logical sense for them to receive any kind of monetary compensation from it whatsoever. People tend to leave that part out.

The irony here is that Barthes argued that the death of the author was also the death of the critic. Since up to Barthes' time the critic was supposed to champion authorial intent, if authorial intent is no longer relevant, then the critic looses their reason to exist. Barthes said that with the intention of "democratizing " the canonical interpretation of a text. An intellectual utopia where every reader's experience of the book would be above everything else.

However, in practice, the opposite happened. The destruction of the author's place in the discourse created an authority vacuum that the critic, which is basically (at least up until recently) a more articulate "reader", got elevated to the status of the actual genius who actually could understand the real meaning of a text better than anyone else. And created a recipe for intellectual dishonesty, and then the Internet and social media turned it into an abject cultural monster.
